coredata.dc:title: Construction of novel Z-scheme nitrogen-doped carbon dots/{0 0 1} TiO2 nanosheet photocatalysts for broad-spectrum-driven diclofenac degradation: Mechanism insight, products and effects of natural water matrices
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fengliang
Wu, Yuliang
Wang, Yingfei
Li, Jiehua
Jin, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Qianxin
Li, Ruobai
Yan, Shichao
Liu, Haijin
Feng, Yiping
Liu, Guoguang
Lv, Wenying
coredata.dc:description:
The development of broad spectrum UV–visible-near-infrared (NIR) photocatalysts is a primary strategy in the area of photocatalytically mediated pollutant elimination. Herein, we report on a novel broad-spectrum N-doped carbon quantum dot/TiO2 nanosheet with higher surface energy {0 0 1} faceted (NCDS/TNS-001) composites with significantly improved broad-spectrum utilization. Under UV, visible, and near infrared light irradiation, NCDS/TNS-001 exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than those of TNS-001, NCDs/P25, and NCDs/TiO2 with {1 0 1} facets (NCDs/T-101) toward the degradation of diclofenac (DCF). This excellent photocatalytic performance might be attributed to the synergistic effects of the highly active {0 0 1} facets, up-converted fluorescent properties of NCDs, and efficient charge separation induced by fabricated heterostructures. The determination of reactive species revealed that O2
− was the dominant species during the photocatalytic degradation of DCF. Together with the analysis of the band structure, a potential Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism for NCDs/TNS-001 was deduced. Photocatalytic pathways of the DCF were further proposed based on intermediate identification and TOC detection, which involved decarboxylation, hydroxylation, and ring opening reactions. The sufficient degradation of DCF in natural water matrices indicated that using NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process for the treatment of ambient DCF wastewater is technical possible.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanosheet
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Diclofenac
Z-scheme heterojunction
Natural water matrices

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Natural water sample collection and preparation
Effects of pH on the degradation of DCF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) HRTEM image, (C) corresponding fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern, and (D) size distribution of NCDs; (E) TEM image and (F) HRTEM image of TNS-001; (G) TEM image, (H) HRTEM image, and elemental mapping of NCDs/TNS-001.

(A) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and (B) XRD spectra of TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001; (C) FT-IR spectra of NCDs, TNS-001, and NCDs/TNS-001; (D) XPS spectrum and (E) high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of NCDs/TNS-001; (F) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Ti2p (Inset: O1s) of TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001; (B) up-converted photoluminescence spectra of NCDs; (C) Photoluminescence spectra, (D) time-resolved fluorescence spectra, (E) transient photocurrent response, and (F) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001.

(A) Photocatalytic activity of the photocatalysts based on the photocatalytic degradation of DCF under visible light irradiation; B) Kinetic rate constant of DCF over different light source irradiation.

ESR spectra of the (A) DMPO-O2 ̇− adducts and (B) DMPO-̇OH recorded with the TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001 under visible light irradiation; (C) Band gaps and (D) VB-XPS spectra of TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001; (E) Schematic band structure evolution of NCDs/TNS-001.
The mineralization of DCF during NCDs/TNS-001 photocatalytic degradation process.
Possible transformation pathways of DCF in the aqueous NCDs/TNS-001 solution under visible light irradiation.
Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the NCDs/TNS-001 under broad- spectrum light irradiation.

Kinetic rate constant of DCF over different (A) pH value, (B) ions and humic acid ([ions] = 5.0 mmol/L, [HA]=10.0 mg/L), and (C) water matrices; (D) Recycling runs of the photocatalytic activity of NCDs/TNS-001 toward DCF degradation.
Scavengers used, RSs quenched, and rate constants with quenched reactive species.

head.ce:title: Construction of novel Z-scheme nitrogen-doped carbon dots/{0 0 1} TiO2 nanosheet photocatalysts for broad-spectrum-driven diclofenac degradation: Mechanism insight, products and effects of natural water matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huaihe River Water Environment and Pollution Control, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huaihe River Water Environment and Pollution Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The development of broad spectrum UV–visible-near-infrared (NIR) photocatalysts is a primary strategy in the area of photocatalytically mediated pollutant elimination. Herein, we report on a novel broad-spectrum N-doped carbon quantum dot/TiO2 nanosheet with higher surface energy {0 0 1} faceted (NCDS/TNS-001) composites with significantly improved broad-spectrum utilization. Under UV, visible, and near infrared light irradiation, NCDS/TNS-001 exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than those of TNS-001, NCDs/P25, and NCDs/TiO2 with {1 0 1} facets (NCDs/T-101) toward the degradation of diclofenac (DCF). This excellent photocatalytic performance might be attributed to the synergistic effects of the highly active {0 0 1} facets, up-converted fluorescent properties of NCDs, and efficient charge separation induced by fabricated heterostructures. The determination of reactive species revealed that O2
− was the dominant species during the photocatalytic degradation of DCF. Together with the analysis of the band structure, a potential Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism for NCDs/TNS-001 was deduced. Photocatalytic pathways of the DCF were further proposed based on intermediate identification and TOC detection, which involved decarboxylation, hydroxylation, and ring opening reactions. The sufficient degradation of DCF in natural water matrices indicated that using NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process for the treatment of ambient DCF wastewater is technical possible.

ce:section.ce:para: As important emerging environmental contaminants, pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) have recently been detected across a wide range of ambient surface waters, groundwater, and drinking water [1,2]. It has been reported that long-term exposure to PPCPs might cause a serious threat to aquatic organisms and human health [3,4]. Among these PPCPs, diclofenac (DCF) is has been widely used to treat pain in musculoskeletal injuries, rheumatoid and osteoarthritis [5]. Due to its extensive usage, DCF has been widely detected in surface water and sewage effluents with a concentration ranging from ng/L to μg/L [6]. Several studies have demonstrated that DCF could affect gill integrity, pituitary gene expression in fish [7,8]. Therefore, the development of effective environmentally compatible DCF treatment techniques is urgently required.

Photocatalytic reactions driven by sunlight are considered to be one of the most promising approaches toward solving the energy crisis and environmental pollution [9–13]. In this regard, the exploration of new photocatalysts that can efficiently operate over a broad-spectrum (UV–Vis–NIR) have garnered worldwide attention. As a prototype photocatalyst, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been widely employed in photocatalytic hydrogen generation and organic contaminant remediation due to its nontoxicity, physical and chemical stability, and earth abundance [14–18]. However, due to its intrinsic wide band-gap energy (ca. 3.2 eV), TiO2 can only utilize UV light at less than 385 nm of the solar-light spectrum [19]. In addition, the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs severely restricts its practical application [20]. Therefore, numerous methods have been adopted to enhance the photoactivity of TiO2, including morphological control [21], deposition of noble metals [22], doping of non-metals [23], loading with carbon materials [24], and coupling with semiconductors [25].

Since photocatalytic reactions occur primarily at the surfaces of catalysts, the morphological control of TiO2 crystal facets has emerged as an efficient technique to enhance its photocatalytic activity [26–28]. Recently, several reports demonstrated that anatase TiO2 with exposed higher surface energy {0 0 1} facets (0.90 J/m2) exhibited higher photocatalytic efficiency than that with lower surface energy {1 0 1} facets (0.44 J/m2) toward organics degradation [29,30]. On one hand, the low atomic coordination numbers of atoms exposed via {0 0 1} facets enabled the easier absorption of reactant molecules compared with {1 0 1} facets [24]. In addition, the electron-hole recombination rates at the grain boundaries of TiO2 nanosheets may be effectively restrained due to its low defect density [31]. Although TiO2 with exposed high energy {0 0 1} facets might effectively improve its photocatalytic activity in the UV light region, poor visible and NIR light utilization continue to hamper its widespread practical application.

As a new type of carbon species, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with dimensions of <10 nm have currently attracted considerable attention owing to their unique attributes, including excellent electron transfer and reservoir properties, unique up-conversion fluorescence capacities, and non-toxicity [32–35]. Accordingly, CQDs have recently been employed to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 nanosheets [24,36]. For example, Yu et al. found that the superior performance of the CQDs/TNS-001 composite toward RhB degradation could be attributed to the unique up-converted photoluminescence behavior and electron transfer capacity of CQDs, as well as the highly activity of {0 0 1} facets [37]. In these studies, CQDs served as an electron-transmitter and optical energy conversion medium of TNS-001. More recently, Zhang et al. found that CQDs could enhance the broad-spectrum sunlight response and restrain the recombination rate of the charge carriers of WO3 through the fabrication of direct Z-scheme heterostructures [38]. Inspired by this, it is plausible that CQDs may also serve as a co-catalyst to improve the broad-spectrum photocatalytic activity of TNS-001 via the construction heterostructures. However, little is known about the heterostructure mechanism of CQDs/TNS-001 composites over the broad spectrum. Additionally, the optical and electrical properties of carbon materials could be significantly improved subsequent to doping with nitrogen atoms, as the nitrogen can induce charge delocalization and alter the work function of carbon [39–41]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the development of NCDs modified TiO2 nanosheets with higher surface energy {0 0 1} facets (NCDs/TNS-001) has never been attempted.

So far, no studies focused on NCDs/TNS-001 photocatalytic elimination of PPCPs in aqueous solutions, especially in natural water matrices. Herein, for the first time, we synthesized broad-spectrum light-driven NCDS/TNS-001 heterostructure photocatalysts via a simple low temperature process, which was applied to the removal of DCF. The morphologies, compositions, as well as optical and electrochemical properties of the photocatalysts were systematically analyzed via multiple characterizations. Subsequently, a potential heterojunction mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of NCDs/TNS-001 was proposed based on band gap structure investigations and the determination of reactive species (RSs). Further, probable DCF photocatalytic degradation pathways were proposed though the products identification by both gas chromatograph and mass spectrometer (GC/MS), and ultraperformance liquid chromatography coupled with a hybrid quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-Q-TOF MS). Finally, the effects of water constituents (ions and humic acid) and different natural waters on the DCF degradation were conducted to assess the technical feasibility of NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process on the treatment of ambient DCF wastewater.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The NCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source [42]. In the experiment, citric acid (1.05 g) and ethylenediamine (0.335 ml) were dispersed in 10 ml of deionized (DI) water (>18 mΩ), which was then transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 5 h. The removal of large particles was achieved through the centrifugation of the NCDs solution, which was then dried at 80 °C for 24 h. Finally, a 10 mg/L NCDs solution was obtained through the redissolution of the NCDs.

TNS-001 was prepared via a hydrothermal method [43]. In detail, 25 ml of tetrabutyl titanate and 4 ml of HF were combined in a Teflon container and stored at 180 °C for 24 h. The white product was then obtained by centrifugation and rinsed several times with ethanol and water. These powders were then soaked in a 700 ml NaOH aqueous solution (0.1 mol/L) for 24 h to remove any absorbed fluorine species, followed by rinsing several times with DI water and drying at 80 °C to obtain the TNS-001. To prepare the TiO2 with exposed {1 0 1} facets (T-101), the same procedure was performed with the use of 4 ml DI water.

A simple low temperature process was employed to synthesize the NCDS/TNS-001 composites, during which 0.25 g of TNS-001 and a certain volume of NCDs solution (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 8.0 ml) were dispersed into a mixed solution of 10 ml DI water and 5 ml ethanol. The mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h, while carefully vaporizing the solvent. The obtained powder was subsequently repeatedly rinsed with water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C. For comparison, NCDs/T-101 and NCDs/P25 were prepared via the identical procedure.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4800), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100HR) were performed to investigate the morphology of the photocatalysts. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by BRUKER D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRAM HR800 Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with a 523 nm laser beam. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was performed on a Nicolet FTIR 6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron Corporation). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained via a Thermo VG ESCALAB 250. UV–vis diffuse reflectance (DRS) spectra were recorded with an UV2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained in a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer. FLS980 fluorescence spectrometry (Edinburgh Instruments) was carried out to obtain the transient photoluminescence decay spectra. Photoelectrochemical measurements were investigated via a CHI660E Electrochemical Workstation (CHI Shanghai, Inc.), in which saturated calomel electrodes (SCE) was employed as the reference electrode. The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. A 50 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300, Beijing Perfectlight Co. ltd) with a 420 nm cut-off filter (100 mW/cm2) was used as the visible light source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Natural water sample collection and preparation
ce:section.ce:para: DI water was generated via a Milli-Q system. River water was collected from the Pearl River in Guangzhou. WWTP effluent water was obtained from a sewage treatment plant (STP) in Guangzhou. Seawater was collected from the South China Sea in Huizhou. All the collected water samples were filtered through a 0.45-μm filter to remove particulate matters and stored at 4 °C for further use. The primary properties of these water samples were summarized in Table S1 of the Supplementary Information.

The photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts were performed on a photoreactor (XPA-7, Nanjing XUJ Co. ltd.). A 350 W Xenon lamp (Nanjing Xujiang electromechanical plant) coupled with a 420 nm cutoff filter (58.6 mW/cm2) was utilized as the visible light source. In order to investigate the broad-spectrum response performance of NCDS/TNS-001 composites, a 350 W Xenon lamp couple with a 290 nm cutoff filter (60.0 mW/cm2), a 750 nm LED monochromatic light (9 W, 36.9 mW/cm2), and a 365 nm LED monochromatic light (3 W, 35.2 mW/cm2, Shenzhen lamplic co., ltd.) were utilized as the simulated sunlight, near infrared light, and UV light sources, respectively. Additionally, a LED light with wavelengths of 450 nm (3 W, 71.6 mW/cm2), 520 nm (3 W, 28.0 mW/cm2), and 660 nm (9 W, 67.4 mW/cm2) were also used to evaluate the activities of the photocatalysts. A 50 mg quantity of photocatalyst was added into the quartz tube, which contained 50 ml 10 mg/L of DCF solution and stirred in dark for 30 min. to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The concentration of the DCF was determined by a high performance liquid chromatography (LC-20A, Shimadzu, Japan) with a diode array detector. Both the 1290UPLC-6540B Q-TOF/MS ultraperformance liquid chromatography coupled with a hybrid quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-Q-TOF MS, Agilent Technologies), and a Trace 1300 gas chromatograph mass spectrometer coupled with TSQ 8000 Evo mass spectrometry (GC–MS, Thermo) were used to detect the photocatalytic degradation products of DCF. Details analytical methods of the HPLC, UPLC-Q-TOF MS, and GC–MS could be found in Text S1 of the Supplementary Information. The total organic carbon (TOC) of the solution was obtained by a Shimadzu TOC-V CPH E200V TOC analyzer.
Electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of reactive species were obtained with a Bruker JES FA200 using 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin-trap reagent.

Reactive species (RSs) scavenging experiments were also performed to investigate the roles of RSs, where 1.0 mM of benzoquinone (BQ) and 10 mM of 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPOL), 10 mM of Na2C2O4, and 10 mM of tertiary butanol (TBA) were used as superoxide iron radical (O2
−), photogenerated hole (h+), and hydroxyl radical (OH) scavengers, respectively [42,44].

The TEM images in Fig. 1 A and 1D demonstrate that the NCDs were spherical with average dimensions of Ø3.0 nm. The lattice spacing of NCDs in the high solution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1B) was 0.208 nm, which could be assigned to the graphite {1 0 0} lattice planes [33]. The fast Fourier transformation (FFT) pattern (Fig. 1C) revealed the hexagonal crystalline structure of the NCDs. Fig. 1E reveals that the TNS-001 samples consisted of nanosheets with a ca. 30–50 nm side length and ca. 6 nm thickness. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1F) indicated that the TNS-001 exhibited the lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.233 for the {0 0 1} planes [21]. Both the TEM and HRTEM images of NCDS/TNS-001 (Fig. 1G and 1H) showed that the NCDs were dispersed on the surface of TNS-001. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of the NCDS/TNS-001 in Fig. 1I to 1L further confirmed that the sample was composed of TiO2 nanosheets and NCDs nanoparticles with obvious C, N, Ti, and O signals.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 in Fig. 2 A revealed that all the samples exhibited a typical IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with the distinct H1 hysteresis loop. The specific surface area of TNS-001 was determined to be 81.04 m2/g, which was similar to the previous studies [45]. Following the introduction of NCDs, the specific surface area of NCDS/TNS-001 decreased slightly from 81.04 m2/g to 71.20 m2/g. This decreasing in specific surface area might be due to the loading of NCDs occupied a part of spaces of TNS-001.

The XRD pattern of the TNS-001 (Fig. 2B) revealed a group of diffraction peaks located at 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.8°, 70.2°, and 75.0°, which corresponded to the {1 0 1}, {0 0 4}, {2 0 0}, {1 0 5}, {2 1 1}, {2 0 4}, {1 1 6}, and {2 2 0} crystal planes of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS card No. 21-1272) [24]. No characteristic diffraction peak of carbon was detected in the NCDS/TNS-001 sample, which was likely due to the small content and low crystallinity of the NCDs in the composites.

Raman spectra of TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Information showed the similar peaks appearing at 136, 387, 509, and 626 cm−1, corresponding to the Eg, B1g, A1g, and Eg modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [36]. However, no obvious D or G bands were detected in the Raman spectra of NCDS/TNS-001, which might be due to the low carbon-lattice-structure content of CDs [46].

In the FT-IR spectrum of the NCDs (Fig. 2C), several peaks from between 1000 cm−1 and 2000 cm−1, as well as a wide peak at ∼3400 cm−1 may be observed, which indicated the presence of carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amide groups on the surfaces of the NCDs [20]. The TNS-001 spectra displayed absorption peaks at 3417 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1, which were attributed to the vibrations of surface adsorbed water and hydroxyl groups, and the bending vibrations of OH groups, respectively [36]. Following the loading of NCDs, a new peaks at 1150 cm−1 of C-O stretching vibration were observed in NCDs/TNS-001 composites [20]. In addition, peaks at 3417 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 showed slightly red-shift after the introduction of NCDs, which implied successful interactions between the TNS-001 and NCDs [36,47,48].

The chemical interactions between the TNS-001 and NCDs were further investigated by XPS analysis. The full XPS spectra of the NCDS/TNS-001 in Fig. 2D revealed the existence of Ti, C, N, and O elements in the composites. The presence of C and N elements indicated the existence of NCDs on the surface of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites. The existence of N also confirmed that nitrogen-atoms were doped within the matrices of the carbon quantum dots. High-resolution C 1s XPS data (Fig. 2E) were fitted by three peaks at binding energies of 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.4 eV, which were assigned to CC, CO, and CN, respectively [49,50]. The corresponding Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 2F) showed two peaks at 458.5 eV and 464.3 eV, which were ascribed to the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 [51]. The O1s spectrum of NCDS/TNS-001 (Inset of Fig. 2F) could be resolved via two peaks that were centered at 529.7 eV and 531.2 eV, which corresponded to TiO and CO [52]. Moreover, both the Ti2p and O1s spectra in the NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited slight negative shifts in contrast to TNS-001, which indicated that the surface chemical environments of Ti and O in the NCDS/TNS-001 composites were altered due to the presence of strong interactions between the TNS-001 and NCDs. The combined results of the XPS analysis and FT-IR measurements suggested that the chemical interactions between the TNS-001 and NCDs were efficiently formed.

UV−vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) in Fig. 3 A revealed TNS-001 absorption edges of ∼400 nm. Subsequent to coupling with NCDs, the absorption edge of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited a significant red-shift, from 400 nm to 458 nm. In addition, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites revealed intense and wide absorption across the UV to visible region, which indicated that the addition of NCDs efficiently increased the adsorption of the TNS-001 nanosheets. The up-converted PL spectra (Fig. 3B) of the NCDs indicated that the NCDs could emit short wavelengths of light, spanning from 365 nm to 650 nm when absorb longer wavelength light, from 450 nm to 850 nm, which revealed that the NCDs showed superior fluorescent up-conversion properties. This excellent fluorescent up-conversion properties of the NCDs enabled them to be an efficient energy transfer material for the transfer of low energy visible light and NIR light to shorter high energy wavelengths for TNS-001 absorption.

Fig. 3C shows the PL spectra of TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 composites under excitation at 325 nm. Compared with TNS-001, NCDs/TNS-001 showed a significantly reduced emission intensity, which indicated that the addition of NCDs hindered the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [53]. The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra for TNS-001 and NCDs/TNS-001 composites were further investigated at 400 nm using 325 nm lasers. The lifetime (τ) of the TNS-001 was 1.3275 ns, which increased to 1.6080 ns for the NCDS/TNS-001 composites (Fig. 3D). The extended lifetime of photo-induced carriers implied that the introduction of the NCDs could promote electron transfer [54]. These results were further confirmed by photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements. As anticipated, the photocurrent intensity of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites was significantly higher than that of TNS-001 (Fig. 3E), which suggested a higher separation and transfer efficiency of the photo-induced carriers in the NCDS/TNS-001 composites. Moreover, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites demonstrated smaller hemicycles in the EIS Nyquist plot than did the TNS-001 (Fig. 3F), which signified that the NCDS/TNS-001 had superior electronic conductivity [42]. Therefore, both the results of PL spectra and electrochemical analysis demonstrated that the transfer and separation of photo-induced charge of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites were significantly improved after coupling with the NCDs.

The photocatalytic performance of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites were evaluated through the degradation of DCF under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 A, after the visible light irradiation for 60 min., a negligible degradation of DCF was observed without photocatalysts, indicating that the DCF was photo-chemically stable. Dark adsorption experiments showed that the adsorption-desorption equilibrium of DCF could be obtained after 30 min. Specifically, 5.2% of DCF was removed by NCDS/TNS-001 after 30 min. of dark adsorption, which was fewer than that by TNS-001 (7.5%). The adsorption results were well coincided with the result of BET. In addition, only 15.4% of the DCF was degraded in the presence of TNS-001 after 60 min. of visible light irradiation. Significantly, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited far more intense photocatalytic activities than did TNS-001. Approximately 91.5% of the DCF was removed over the same timeline. The NCDS/TNS-001 also showed enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to the NCDs/T-101 and NCDs/P25, which revealed the high oxidation activity of the exposed {0 0 1} facets. Further, by increasing the added volume of the NCDs solution from 0.0 to 2.0 ml, the pseudo first-order rate constants (kobs) of the DCF increased from 0.0016 to 0.0376 min−1 (Fig. S2 of the Supplementary Information). However, further increases in the NCDs solution resulted in a decrease in degradation efficacy, which might due to the photo screening effect of the NCDs [37].

To assess whether the NCDS/TNS-001 composites could be used to the degradation of DCF under broad-spectrum irradiation, the degradation of DCF was carried out under visible light, NIR light, UV light, and simulated sunlight irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4B, an extremely poor DCF degradation efficiency was observed by TNS-001 under 365 nm UV light irradiation, and insignificant DCF removal was obtained under visible light, 450 nm monochromatic light, 520 nm monochromatic light, 660 nm monochromatic light, and 750 nm monochromatic NIR light irradiation. In contrast, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited significant activity for the photocatalytic degradation of DCF under each of these light sources. Further, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites demonstrated excellent DCF degradation activity under simulated sunlight irradiation. These results indicated that combining NCDs with TNS-001 could yield responsive broad-spectrum photocatalysts.

To study the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites, an ESR technique was carried out using DMPO as the spin-trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5B, under visible light irradiation, the weak ESR signals for DMPO-.OH and DMPO-O2
− were observed in the TNS-001, which indicated the generation of .OH and O2
− during the TNS-001 photocatalytic system. Additionally, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited stronger signals for both DMPO-.OH and DMPO-O2
− than for pure TNS-001, implying that additional .OH and O2
− could be generated after coupling with the NCDs. To further examine the key reactive species in the NCDS/TNS-001 composite photocatalytic system, scavenger-quenching experiments were conducted through addition of various scavengers. As shown in Table 1 , the addition of TBA resulted in a slight inhibitory effect in DCF degradation, with an inhibition rate of 14.6%. However, when BQ and TEMPOL (as O2
− scavengers) were applied, the DCF photodegradation process was almost restricted. The results of the scavenger-quenching experiments indicated that O2 .− was the dominant species during the photocatalytic NCDS/TNS-001 mediated degradation of DCF. Previous studies revealed that O2
− could be produced via conduction band electrons through the trapping of dissolved oxygen [34]. To ascertain the role of O2, degradation of DCF was performed in N2 and O2 condition. A dramatic decrease in activity was observed under bubbling with N2, while a significant increased effect was noted under bubbling with O2. These results verified that O2 was actively engaged and played an important role in the oxidation reaction and might be involved in the degradation system through the generation of O2
−. Interestingly, the photodegradation process was enhanced when Na2C2O4 (as h+ scavenger) was applied, which implied the insignificant role of h+ during the photocatalytic process.

To elucidate the generation mechanism of reactive species, the energy and structure of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites was determined. Fig. 5C depicts the optical band gap (Eg) of the TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 as derived from the Tauc equation [55]. The Eg of TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 were estimated to be 3.1 eV and 2.7 eV, respectively. The NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited a narrower band gap than did TNS-001, which was beneficial for the absorption of the sunlight [56]. To gain a better understanding of energy band structures of the TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001, VB-XPS spectra were employed to determine the VB positions. In Fig. 5D, it can be seen that the VB of both TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 were calculated to be 2.7 eV. The CB positions of the TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 were subsequently estimated to be −0.4 eV, and 0 eV, respectively, according to Ec = Ev − Eg. The band energy diagrams of the TNS-001 and NCDS/TNS-001 are shown in Fig. 5E. Therefore, the initial types of possible energy band structures of the NCDS/TNS-001 were deduced; specifically, the electron accumulation effect of the NCDs resulted in the shifting of the CB position of the TNS-001, from −0.4 eV to 0 eV [57]. According to this type of NCDS/TNS-001 band structure, the CB position of the NCDS/TNS-001 (0 eV) was far more positive than the reduction potential of O2/O2
− (−0.33 eV), which implied that the photogenerated electrons in the CB of the NCDS/TNS-001 was thermodynamically unable to reduce the O2 to O2
− [33]. However, both the results of the ESR technique and scavenger-quenching experiments revealed that a large amount of O2
− was generated in the NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic system. This result suggested that the NCDs might have served as co-catalysts that occupied an appropriate CB position to reduce O2 to O2
− [38]. Actually, the CB and VB potentials of hexagonal NCDs were established as −1.99 eV and 0.41 eV, which indicated that heterojunctions might have been formed between the TNS-001 and NCDs [58]. Considering the band gap levels of TNS-001 and NCDs, the charge separation might be estimated to proceed via two possible mechanisms: conventional type-II and direct Z-scheme heterojunction. As shown in Fig. 6 E, in a conventional type II heterojunction, the excited electrons in the CB of the NCDs would transfer to the CB of the TNS-001, while the holes in the VB of the TNS-001 would transfer to the VB of the NCDs [59]. This means that the electrons would finally accumulate on the CB of the TNS-001, and the holes on the VB of the NCDs. In this case, the holes accumulated on the VB of NCDs would not oxidize OH− to OH, as the VB position of the NCDs (0.41 eV) would be far more negative than the standard redox potential of OH/OH− (1.99 eV vs NHE) [60]. However, the identification of reactive species revealed that OH was generated in the NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic system, which suggested that the type-II heterostructure might not applicable to the heterostructured NCDS/TNS-001 system. On the contrary, in a direct Z-scheme heterojunction, the CB excited electrons of the TNS-001 would combine with the VB holes of the NCDs, leading to an electron reserve on the CB of the NCDs and a hole reserve on the VB of the NCDs [49]. Since the CB position of the NCDs (−1.99 eV) was more negative than the potentials of O2
−/O2 (−0.33 eV), and the VB position of TNS-001 (2.7 eV) was more positive than the potentials of .OH/OH− (1.99 eV), both OH and O2
− might be produced in the NCDS/TNS-001 heterostructure system. Therefore, it may be inferred that the NCDS/TNS-001 composites might follow the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism.

The transformation intermediates of DCF generated during the NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process were identified by UPLC-Q-TOF MS and GC–MS. Figs. S3 and S4 in the Supplementary Data reveals the MS fragmentation patterns of the DCF reaction sample, in which eight intermediates with different m/z and the parent DCF (m/z = 294) were observed. Among them, intermediates with m/z 267, 250, and 281 were identified as decarboxylation products, while the intermediates with m/z 310 and 325 were assigned as hydroxylation products. Intermediates with m/z 168, 166, and 150 were recognized as the ring opening products of DCF.

The TOC removal rate was investigated to elucidate the final fate of DCF in the NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process. It was found that ca. 51.4% of the TOC can be removed after 60 min. of visible light irradiation (Fig. 6). This result implied that DCF and its photocatalytic degradation intermediates would be attacked by a series of RSs and finally mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

Based on the above results, a plausible photocatalytic DCF degradation pathway was proposed (Fig. 7 ). Our previous research confirmed that the DCF resident C-15 possessed a higher positive point charge, which implied that the carboxyl-C in DCF might be susceptible to O2 .− and e− attack [61]. Therefore, pathway I was initiated by O2 .− that attacked the carboxyl-C/C5, which resulted in the cleavage of carboxyl; thereby generating a carbon-centered radical. This carbon-centered radical was further attacked by OH to generate m/z 267 or undergo a hydrogen shift that led to the m/z 251 product. The second degradation pathway was initiated by e−, which attacked the C-15 in DCF to form m/z 281. Another degradation step occurred via a OH attack on the aromatic ring, resulting in the generation of a m/z 311 hydroxylation product. Subsequently, the further hydroxylation of m/z 310 might give rise to a dihydroxylation product, which could be further oxidized to a m/z 325 quinone imine structured product. These byproducts might be additionally oxidized to lead to the ring opening products (m/z 182, 166, and 150), and finally be mineralized into H2O and CO2 following prolonged irradiation.

Based on the above results, a complete photocatalytic mechanism for the NCDS/TNS-001 composites under broad-spectrum irradiation was deduced, as showed in Fig. 8 . Under broad-spectrum irradiation, visible light and near-infrared light can be absorbed by the NCDs, leading to the formation of electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, longer-wavelength visible and NIR light could be efficiently transformed by the NCDs to shorter-wavelength light (365 nm to 650 nm) due to its superior up-conversion property. The shorter wavelengths of less than 400 nm could excite TNS-001 to generate electron-hole pairs. Due to the formation of direct Z-scheme heterostructures between TNS-001 and NCDs, the electrons in the CB of the TNS-001 would combine with the holes in the VB of the NCDs. As a result, the surface electron-hole pair density of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites were increased; hence, leading to an enhanced photocatalytic capacity. Because of the appropriate CB position of the NCDs and the VB position of the TNS-001, the electrons in the CB of the NCDs could readily combine with oxygen on the surface of the composites to form O2
−, whereas the h+ in the VB of the TNS-001 could react with H2O/OH− to produce OH. Finally, the DCF in the solution was adsorbed onto the surface of NCDS/TNS-001 composites, and attacked by the generated RSs, particularly O2
−, which resulted in complete decomposition and mineralization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on the degradation of DCF
ce:section.ce:para: pH was considered to be a critical role during the photocatalytic process, as it may impact the surface charges of photocatalysts, the ionization states of organic compounds, as well as the formation of RSs [62]. It can be observed in Fig. 9 A that the reaction rate constant decreased dramatically from 0.0918 min−1 at pH 3.0 to 0.0376 min−1 to at pH 7.0, then increased when pH further increased from 7.0 to 9.0. However, further increased the pH to 11.0 lead to the dramatic decreased in the reaction rate. The point of zero charge of NCDs/TNS-001 was ca. 6.3, whereas the dissociation constants (pKa) of DCF was 4.35 [63,64]. An electrostatic attraction between NCDs/TNS-001 and DCF might have occurred when the pH of the solution was between 4.35 and 6.3, which might lead to strong absorption between NCDs/TNS-001 and DCF; thus accelerating the activity of the NCDs/TNS-001 system. On the contrary, electrostatic repulsion might occur when the pH exceeds 6.3 and within 4.35, leading to low reaction activity. Consequently, it is not surprised that low degradation rates for DCF degradation was observed when the pH of the solution was 11. Interestingly, NCDs/TNS-001 showed excellent photocatalytic performance toward the degradation of DCF at extreme acidic pH (3.0), which might have been due to the smaller size of photocatalyst at lower pH [65]. At weak alkaline condition (9.0), the addition of OH− could be absorbed on the surface of NCDs/TNS-001, leading to the increasing in the formation of RSs [66]. In summary, NCDs/TNS-001 process showed superior photocatalytic efficiency at a rather wide pH range from extreme acidic to weak alkaline condition, implying that the NCDs/TNS-001 photocatalytic system could be applied for the remediation of DCF-containing wastewater that with a wide range of pH.

Natural water constituents (e.g. cations, anions, and dissolved organic matter (DOM)) were ubiquitously present in natural waters and might affect the photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds [67–69]. It is evident that Cl−, SO4 2−, Mg2+ and Ca2+ exhibit an inapparent influence for the photocatalytic degradation of DCF (Fig. 9B). On the contrary, transition metal ions (Fe3+ and Cu2+) significantly restrain the degradation of DCF. This suppressive effect on the degradation of DCF might have been due to the electrons scavenging effects of transition metal ions, thereby reducing the formation of O2
− [4]. In addition, a representative of aquatic DOM, humic acid (HA) exhibited a significant inhibitory effect on DCF degradation. Generally, the HA was considered as an inner filter, which could weaken the incident light utilized by the NCDS/TNS-001 [70]. Further, HA may also serve as the scavenger of RSs (e.g. OH and O2
−), to thus inhibit the degradation of SMT [71].

Photocatalytic degradation of DCF in different natural waters (DI water, WWTP effluent, pearl river water, and sea water) were then carried out to assess the technical feasibility of NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process on the treatment of ambient DCF wastewater. As showed in Fig. 9C, the degradation of DCF was slightly inhibited in all the natural water matrices when compared to DI water. After 60 min of visible light irradiation, ca. 90.9%, 84.0%, 84.6%, and 84.4% of DCF were eliminated in DI water, WWTP effluent, pearl river water and sea water, respectively. These inhibition effects might be due to the coefficient of co-existing HA and anions that present in water matrices (Table S1 in the Supplementary Data). The excellent removal performance of DCF indicated that NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process could be used for the treatment of ambient DCF wastewater.

Cycling runs for the photodegradation of DCF were carried out to verify the stability of the NCDS/TNS-001 composites. As shown in Fig. 9D, following four consecutive experiments, ca. 80.0% of DCF could be removed, which was slightly lower than that achieved on first use (91.0%). In addition, no significant changes were observed in the FTIR spectra between the fresh and recycled NCDS/TNS-001 composites (Fig. S5 of the Supplementary Data), which suggested the high stability of NCDS/TNS-001 composites.

In summary, for the first time, we successfully synthesized broad-spectrum photocatalytic NCDS/TNS-001 composites via a simple low temperature process. Compared with TNS-001, NCDs/P25, and NCDs/T-101, the NCDS/TNS-001 composites exhibited significantly higher photocatalytic activity toward the degradation of DCF under broad-spectrum irradiation. The robust activity of the {0 0 1} facets, up-converting fluorescent properties of the NCDs, and efficient charge separation led to enhanced photocatalytic performance. The ESR technique and experimental quenching results indicated that O2̇ – were the dominant RSs during the NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process. From the analysis of the band structure, a potential Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism for the NCDs/TNS-001 was deduced. Product identification demonstrated that DCF can be finally mineralized to CO2 and H2O following prolonged irradiation. A slight inhibition on the degradation of DCF was observed in river water, WWTP effluent, and sea water, implying that NCDS/TNS-001 photocatalytic process could be utilization for the treatment of ambient DCF contaminated water. In summary, this study not only provided a new strategy in broad-spectrum photocatalyst design for the remediation of ambient DCF contaminated water, but also advanced our understanding of the Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism of TiO2 based photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21377031 and 21677040) and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2017A050506052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.09.092.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
